sections.label: Agricultural Practices
sections.html:
Agriculture: The applied branch of biology that deals with the cultivation of plants and rearing of animals are called agriculture.

Most plants make their own food through the process of Photosynthesis. Animals feed on plants or other animals to get energy.

When we cultivate the same kind of plants on a large scale in one place, it is called a Crop.

Some crop plants are grown in India.

S.NO. Type of crop Crop produce
1 Cereals Wheat, rice, maize, bajra, barley
2 Pulses Gram, pea, bean, moong
3 Vegetable Cabbage, cauliflower, brinjal
4 Fruits Mango, apple, pear, guava
5 Spices Cardamom, black pepper
6 Fibre crops Cotton, jute, hemp
7 Plantation crops Tea, coffee, rubber, coconut
8 Sugar crops Sugar cane, sugar beet
9 Oil crops Groundnut, mustard, castor
10 Medicinal crops Tulsi, Asparagus, neem
11 Timber Teak, sheesham, pine

Kharif crops: The crops which are sown in the rainy season are called Kharif crops. The growing of Kharif crops starts in June-July at the beginning of southwest monsoon because these crops need substantial amount of water. Examples of kharif crops are paddy, maize, millet, soyabean, groundnut and cotton. Such crops are also called summer crops.

Rabi crops: The crops grown in the winter season are called rabi crops. The time period of rabi crops is generally from October to March. The sowing of rabi crops begins at the beginning of winter (October – November) and the crops are harvested by March or April. Examples of rabi crops are wheat, peas, mustard, and linseed.

sections.label: Basic Practices of Crop Production
sections.html:
Agricultural Practices: All activities which farmers do for the cultivation of plants are called agricultural practices.

The various agricultural practices which are carried out at various stages of crop production are:

  1. Preparation of soil
  2. Sowing
  3. Adding manure and fertilisers
  4. Irrigation
  5. Removal of weeds
  6. Harvesting
  7. Storage of food grains.

Preparation of Soil: To loosen and turn the soil.

Sowing: Planting of seeds of a crop in soil.

Adding Manure and Fertilisers: Adding essential nutrients to soil for growth and development of plants.

Protection from Weeds: Removal of unwanted plants from the cultivated field to allow crops proper access to lights, space, and nutrients.

Harvesting: Cutting mature crops from fields.

Irrigation: Supplying water to plants at regular intervals.

Storage: Keeping grains or produce safe from rats, insects, microorganisms, and moisture.

sections.label: Preparation of Soil
sections.html:
The soil is prepared by ploughing, levelling and manuring.

The process of loosening and turning the soil is called ploughing (tilling). It is done by using an implement called plough.

Advantages of ploughing:

  1. The loose soil allows the plant roots to penetrate freely and deeper into the soil so that plants are held more firmly to the ground.
  2. The loose soil allows the roots of the plants to breathe easily.
  3. The loose soil helps in the growth of worms and microbes present in the soil as they help in turning and loosening of the soil.
  4. Ploughing also uproots and buries the weeds standing in the field and thereby suffocates them to death.

Plough: Plough is a large agricultural implement that is used for ploughing or tilling in the fields.

Hoe: It is an agricultural implement used for removing weeds, and loosening and turning the soil.

Cultivator: The cultivator is a tractor driven agricultural implement which is used for loosening and turning the soil in the fields quickly.

Levelling: After tilling or Ploughing, Levelling is done in which the big clumps of soil called crumbs are broken (and leveled). Leveling the field helps in the process of sowing and irrigation.

Levelling is done for the following reasons:

  1. The ploughed field may have big pieces of soil called crumb which need to be crushed.
  2. Levelling prevents soil erosion. The leveled soil is not blown by the wind or drained by water.
  3. Levelling also helps in the uniform distribution of water and manure.

sections.label: Sowing
sections.html:
Sowing means, putting the seeds in the soil. Before doing so, it is necessary to take care of the quality of the seeds.

Traditional Tool: The tool used traditionally for sowing seeds is funnel-shaped. The seeds are filled into the funnel, passed down through two or three pipes having sharp ends which pierce the soil and place seeds there.

Sowing by hand: The sowing of seeds by hand is called broadcasting. The seeds are taken in hand and gradually scattered in the entire ploughed field. This method is not good as there is no proper spacing or proper depth at which the seeds are sown by hand. The seeds scattered on the surface of the soil for sowing can be picked up and eaten by birds.

Seed Drill: It is an implement used for sowing seeds with the help of tractors. By using a seed drill, the seeds are sown at the correct depth and correct intervals. The seeds sown with a seed drill are in regular rows and get covered by soil. This prevents the seeds to be picked up and eaten by birds.

Advantages of using a seed drill:

  1. By using a seed drill for sowing, the seeds are sown at the correct depth and correct intervals.
  2. The seeds sown with a seed drill are in regular rows.
  3. When the seeds are sown in furrows by a seed drill, the seeds get covered by soil and hence cannot be picked up and eaten by birds.
  4. Since it is tractor driven, it saves time and labour.

Transplanting: In this case, the young plants are developed in the nursery bed and the seedlings are transferred to the field ensuring that there is proper spacing. It is mainly done in the case of paddy crops and bananas.

Advantages of transplanting:

  1. It helps in selecting and planting only healthy seedings.
  2. Spacing can be controlled because of manual plantation.
  3. Plants get sufficient sunlight, nutrients and water from the soil.

The Ministry of Agriculture in India has set up a national-level organisation called the National Seeds Corporation (NSCC) that tests the quality of seeds.

State Seeds Corporations and Agricultural Universities have also set up seed testing laboratories throughout India. These labs test seeds for their:

  • purity,
  • resistance to diseases and pests,
  • germination and vigour,
  • suitability to regional climatic conditions, and
  • general seed health

sections.label: Adding Manure and Fertilisers
sections.html:
Manure is a natural substance obtained by the decomposition of animal wastes like cow dung, human wastes, and plant residues, which supplies essential elements and humus to the soil and makes it more fertile.

A fertilizer is a salt or an organic compound containing the necessary plant nutrients like nitrogen, phosphorous or potassium, to make the soil more fertile.

Natural Organic Manure: This includes raw manure, compost, and green manure: Raw manure is a mixture of cattle and domestic waste. Compost is well-rotted plant and animal residue. Green Manure is crops grown in the field as the pure crop or as an intercrop between the main crops - and then, buried in the field to enrich the soil.

Biofertilizers: These are the nitrogen-fixing organisms that are widely used in organic farming and make agriculture sustainable. These include Rhizobium, Azotobacter, blue-green algae, and Mycorrizae (a type of fungi that increases phosphorus uptake in fruit crops like papaya and citrus fruits).

Vermi-Compost: It is a type of compost that is made using earthworms.

The excessive use of fertilisers is harmful due to the following reasons:

  1. It changes the chemical nature of the soil and makes it less fertile.
  2. The excessive use of fertilisers causes water pollution in ponds, lakes, rivers, etc.

Differences between manures and fertilisers:

Manures Fertilisers
Manure is a natural substance obtained by the decomposition of animal wastes like cow dung, human waste, and plant residues. A fertilizer is a salt or an organic compound.
It is not very rich in essential plant nutrients like nitrogen, phosphorous, and potassium. It is very rich in essential plant nutrients like nitrogen, phosphorous, and potassium.
It provides a lot of organic matter like humus to the soil. These are prepared in factories.

Advantages of manure:

  1. It enhances the water-holding capacity of the soil.
  2. It makes the soil porous due to which the exchange of gases becomes easy.
  3. It increases the number of useful microbes in the soil.
  4. It improves the texture of the soil.

Crop rotation: The practice in which different types of crops (leguminous and non-leguminous) are grown alternately in the same field or soil is called crop rotation. In crop rotation, the cereal crops like wheat, maize, paddy, and millet are grown with leguminous crops like pulses, beans, peas, etc. in the same field.

Manures are divided into three different types:

  1. Farm yard manure - This type of manure consists of dung, urine, leaves, and other farmyard wastes.
  2. Compost - This type of manure is obtained by the decomposition of dead plants and animal wastes, sewage waste, etc. All the organic material is buried in a pit with alternative layers of soil and is allowed to rot.
  3. Green manure - This type of manure is formed by the decomposition of leguminous plants like sunhemp and guar. These plants are grown and sown back into the same soil.

 

Some example of fertilizers are:

  1. Nitrogenous fertilizers- Urea, ammonium, sulphate, sodium nitrate
  2. Phosphatic fertilizers: Superphosphate, ammonium phosphate.
  3. Potassic fertilizers: Potash , potassium silphate
  4. Complex fertilizers: nitrophosphate, urea, ammonium phosphate.

sections.label: Irrigation
sections.html:
The methods of irrigation are distributed as:

  • Traditional methods of irrigation
  • Modern methods of irrigation

The various traditional methods of irrigation are:

  1. Moat method (Pulley system)
  2. Chain pump
  3. Dhekli
  4. Rahat (Lever system)

Modern Methods of Irrigation

  • Sprinkler System
  • Drip System

Sprinkler system: In a sprinkler system of irrigation, the main pipe is laid in the fields to which perpendicular pipes are joined at regular intervals.

Drip system: In the drip system, there is a network of narrow pipes with small holes in the fields. When water falls through these narrow pipes, it falls drop by drop at the position of the roots of the plants. There is no run-off or wastage of water. This technique is the best technique for watering fruit plants, trees, and gardens.

The advantages of drip irrigation system are:

  • It provides water to the plants drop by drop so water is not wasted at all.
  • It minimizes the use of water in agriculture and is useful in those areas where the availability of water is poor.

 

Crop based Irrigation: Some crop plants require more water while others need less water. For example, rice crops required a continuous water supply whereas wheat and cotton require less water.

Soil Based irrigation:

  • Irrigation also depends upon the nature of the soil in which the crop is grown.
  • Sandy soil needs frequent irrigation due to its poor water retaining capacity.
  • Clayey soil needs less frequent irrigation due to its good water retaining capacity.

Season-based Irrigation: In summer the frequency of irrigation is higher. It is due to the increased rate of evaporation of water from the soil and the leaves.

Advantages of water to plants:

  • The plant body has about 90% of water in it.
  • It protects the plant body from frost in winters and withering in hot summers.
  • It is needed for the germination of seeds, growth of plants, and absorption of minerals in the soil.
  • It is needed for transporting minerals and food within the plant's body.
  • It keeps the plant body cool during summer by transpiration.

Disadvantages of excess water to plants.

  • It decreases the penetrating of air into the soil and thus decreases the availability of air to roots.
  • Increase the salt content in the soil.

sections.label: Protection from Weeds
sections.html:
Undesirable plants that grow naturally along with the crop are called weeds. Removal of weeds is called weeding.

The various methods of weeding are as following:

  1. Removal of weeds by pulling them out with hand.
  2. Removal of weeds by using a trowel (khurpa).
  3. Destroying the weeds by spraying special chemicals called weedicides. Some of the common weedicides are 2.4-D, MCPA and Butachlor. These are sprayed in the fields to kill weeds and do not damage crops.

Removal of weeds is essential because:

  • Weeds compete with crops for space, light, water, and nutrients.
  • They may interfere in harvesting and can be poisonous for animals and human beings.

What should farmers keep in mind while using weedicides?

  • The best time to remove weeds is before they produce flowers and seeds.
  • Weedicides are diluted with water and sprayed in the fields. Farmers should cover their noses and mouth with a piece of cloth while spraying them.

 

Disadvantages associated with weedicides and pesticides:

  • Accidental contact with these chemicals may cause harm to the farmer’s health, so he keeps his mouth and nose properly covered while applying the chemical.
  • Traces of these chemicals remain on the surface of fruit and vegetables, which can be consumed by humans and may harm them. So, it is highly advisable to wash vegetables and fruits thoroughly before consuming them.

sections.label: Harvesting
sections.html:
The cutting and gathering of the matured food crop is called harvesting.

In harvesting, the crops like wheat or rice are cut close to the ground by hand using a cutting tool called sickle. This is called manual harvesting.

Threshing is separating chaff from grain by beating the crop against a stone or wooden bar. In this process, grains fall from the stalk due to force.

After threshing, winnowing separates the husk from the seeds by blowing air through it. In this process, the husk (which is lighter) flies away and the seeds (which are heavier) fall down.

There is a machine called ‘Combine’ which works as a harvester as well as a thresher.

The special festivals in the harvest seasons are Pongal, Baisakhi, Holi, Nabanya and Bihu.

sections.label: Storage
sections.html:
Grains protected from pests, bacteria and fungi

  • Grains (seeds) are dried in the sun to reduce their moisture.
  • On small scale, grains are stored in jute bags or metallic bins.
  • On large scale, they are kept in silos and granaries.
  • Dried neem leaves are used at home to protect food grains.
  • In big godowns, chemical treatments are done to protect large quantities of grain.

 

Precautions to be taken during the storage of food grains are:

  • Grains should be dried properly or they might rot easily.
  • They should be stored in completely dry gunny bags.
  • The bags should be kept in a place that is completely moisture-free.
  • Storage areas should be well-ventilated.
  • In larger godowns, care should be taken that chemicals used to repel or kill insects and rats do not contaminate food grains.

The large scale storage of food grains is stored in either gunny bags or grain silos.

sections.label: Food from Animals
sections.html:
The animals which provide us food are mainly of two types:

  1. Milk yielding animals. Examples - cow, buffalo and goat.
  2. Meat and egg yielding animals. Examples - goat, sheep, fish and poultry.

When animals are reared at a large scale to obtain food from them, it is called Animal Husbandry.

Animal husbandry includes taking care of animals, breeding them, and domesticating them for different purposes such as meat, wool, milk, eggs, honey etc.

Types of animal husbandry popular in India include:

  • Beekeeping or Apiculture
  • Cattle farming
  • Dairy farming
  • Fish Farming or Aquaculture
  • Poultry farming
  • Sheep farming

Breeding means mating animals with superior characters to create a new breed (or offspring that is more useful to us than its parents). Breeding can be of two types:

  • Inbreeding
  • Outbreeding

Advantages of inbreeding:
Inbreeding allows us to eliminate the harmful recessive genes in a breed and selectively choose and nurture superior genes. In the case of cattle, a superior female produces more milk per lactation while a superior male produces superior progeny than other males.

Out-crossing: When animals of the same breed are mated together but have no common ancestors (on either side of the pedigree) for four to six generations, it is called Outcrossing. The resultant offspring is called Outcross.

Cross Breeding: When superior males of one breed are mated with superior males of another breed, it is called Cross Breeding. This helps scientists to combine the desirable qualities of the two breeds. In Punjab, Bikaneri ewes were mated with Marino rams to create a new breed of sheep called Hisardale.

Interspecific Hybridization: When a male and a female of two different species of animals are mated together, it is called Hybridization. For Example, when a donkey and a horse is mated, a new breed called Mule is born.

sections.label: Microorganisms
sections.html:
Microorganisms are found in the following five major groups:

  1. Bacteria
  2. Viruses
  3. Algae
  4. Fungi
  5. Protozoa

Bacteria:

  • Bacteria are very small and simplest living organisms.
  • The size of bacteria is 0.2 to 100 microrons.
  • It is single-celled organism found everywhere.

 

Bacteria are found in four different shapes.

  1. Bacilli (rod-shaped bacteria) e.g. Escherichia coli
  2. Cooci ( spherical bacteria) e.g. Streptococcus pneumonia
  3. Vibrio (common shaped) e.g. Vibrio cholera
  4. Spirilla (spiral-shaped bacteria) e.g. Treponema pallidum

The bacteria which need oxygen for growth are called aerobic bacteria.

The bacteria which survive without oxygen are called anaerobic bacteria.

Useful Bacteria: Lactobacillus (curd bacteria), Acetobacter acetic (vinegar bacteria), E. coil (inside our body).

  • They help in making manure, minerals in the soil, and fixation of nitrogen in the soil.
  • They help in making medicines (antibiotics).
  • They are needed to make curd and cheese, vinegar, alcohol, tanning of leather, rotting of flax, ripening of tea leaves, etc.

 

Harmful Bacteria: Clostridium botulinum, Salmonella typhi, vibrio choleras, Bacillus coil

  • Bacteria cause diseases like typhoid, cholera, diarrhoea.
  • The food that is spoiled or stale must not be eaten.

 

Viruses:

  • The viruses mean poisonous liquid.
  • Viruses are the smallest microorganisms
    Examples: vaccinia virus, influenza virus, HIV-I virus and poliovirus.

Viruses:

  • They can be only studied under an electron microscope.
  • They are cellular which means they do not have any of the substances present in a cell.
  • Viruses can not grow and reproduce on their own.
  • Viruses are said to be a connecting link between living things and non-living things.

Useful Viruses:
Viruses are used for scientific research such as in biotechnology, genetic engineering, gene therapy, making antibiotics, insect-killing viruses, etc.

Harmful viruses:

  1. Viruses cause diseases like smallpox, the common cold, influenza, AIDS, herpes, hepatitis, polio, and rabies.
  2. Some viruses are also implicated in the development of cancer.
  3. Plant viral diseases include various forms of yellowing and blistering of leaves and stems.
  4. Antiviral drugs are effective against certain viral diseases and vaccines if available, provide protection against others.

Algae

  • They are unicellular as well as multicellular.
  • They have no stem, roots and leaves like other plants but they have chlorophyll and they can make their food by photosynthesis.

Useful Algae:

  • Some algae are used as food sources as they provide carbohydrates, vitamins (A, C, D, E), and proteins.
  • In industry, they are used in glass, porcelain, metal paints, varnish, polish, toothpaste, and cosmetics manufacturing.
  • Algae fix nitrogen.
  • Algae products like shells of diatoms give a gritty texture to toothpaste.

 

Harmful Algae:
The harmful effects of algae can be seen as algal bloom in the water. The rapid growth of algae causes poisonous effects which result in the death of aquatic organisms due to a lack of oxygen in the water. They also release toxins into the water.

Fungi:

  • Fungi are a group of diverse organisms that lack chlorophyll.
  • Fungi generally grow in dark, warm, and moist conditions.
  • Some fungi are saprophytes and some are parasites.

 

Fungi: Fungi are unicellular as well as multi-cellular. Example: yeast, mushroom, penicillium, mould etc.

Useful Fungi:

  1. The yeast used to make the dough in bakery, alcohol, idli, and dhokla by their termination.
  2. Penicillium is used to make antibiotics called Penicillium.
  3. Mushrooms and moulds are used to make manure, vitamins, and organic acids.
  4. Mushroom is eaten in curry, soup, and pulao. All mushrooms are not edible.

 

Harmful Fungi:

  1. Fungi cause a large number of diseases in plants and animals such as rust disease in plants, fruit not all of the apple, the early blight of potato, red rot of sugarcane, ringworm disease of human beings, etc.
  2. Fungi can cause skin infections, scalp, and nails in human beings.
  3. Moulds spoil food if kept in warm and moist places.
  4. Aspergillus is a fungus that spoils jellies, bread, pickles, etc.
  5. Fungicides are chemicals that are used to kill or destroy the fungus.

 

Protozoa:

  • Protozoan means the first animal.
  • Protozoa are unicellular microorganisms.
  • Protozoa are found in ponds, lakes, rivers, sea, and damp soil.

 

Protozoa:

  • Protozoa are either saprophytic or parasitic.
  • Euglena contains chlorophyll and prepared its own food.
    Example: Amoeba, Paramecium, Euglena, Trypanosoma.

Useful Protozoa:

  1. Some forms of protozoa help in the final degradation of waste and sewage.
  2. Some are symbiotic in nature, thus helping to benefit other organisms in many ways. Protozoa are used as research material for various biological activities.

 

Harmful Protozoa:

  1. E.histolytica lives in the large intestine of humans and causes Amoebic dysentery.
  2. E.gingivalis causes pyorrhea, infection of the gums, and bad breath.
  3. Plasmodium causes malaria fever.
  4. Trypanosoma causes sleeping sickness.

sections.label: Where do Microorganisms Live?
sections.html:
Some organisms grow in groups called colonies. Microorganisms like fungi and bacteria may live in colonies.

Microorganism are found in air, soil, and in water bodies like seas, lakes, and ponds.

Microorganisms are also found in dead and decaying organisms and even inside our bodies.

Microorganisms can exist in extreme conditions of temperature like drought, in hot water springs, in deserts, polar regions, and marshy lands.

Microorganisms survive in extreme environmental conditions by forming a hard outlet covering called cyst.

Microorganisms like amoeba can live alone.

sections.label: Microorganisms and Us
sections.html:
Lactobacillus bacteria help make curd from milk.

Bacteria act upon tough meat and make it soft. This process is called tenderization.

Yeast cells are used in the baking industry for making bread, pastries, and cakes.

Agar-agar a jelly-like substance, obtained from red algae Gellidium and Gracillaria is used in the preparation of various food items like ice creams, jellies, and puddings.

Yeast is used for the commercial production of alcohol, beer, wine, and acetic acid. The process of conversion of sugar into alcohol by the action of microbes like yeast, is called fermentation. Louis Pasteur discovered the process of fermentation in 1857.

Production of antibiotics: The medicines produced by living organisms such as bacteria and fungi which kill or stop the growth of the disease-causing microorganisms present in our body are called antibiotics.
Streptomycin, tetracycline, aureomycin, and chloromycetin are some commonly used antibiotics that are made from fungi and bacteria.

The first antibiotic was penicillin which was prepared by a fungus called Penicillium notatum.

Precautions to be taken while using antibiotics:

  1. Antibiotics should be taken only on the advice of a qualified doctor.
  2. We must complete the entire course of antibiotics.
  3. We should not take antibiotics when not needed or in the wrong doses.
  4. Antibiotics taken unnecessarily may kill the beneficial bacteria in the body.
  5. Antibiotics should not be taken when we are suffering from cold and flu.

The ability of one’s body to resist disease is known as immunity.

Immunity against a particular disease can be developed by a technique called vaccination.

A small dose of dead and weakened disease-causing microbes used to stimulate an immune response in the body is called vaccine.

Cholera, tuberculosis, smallpox, tetanus, polio, and hepatitis B can be prevented by vaccination. Vaccination can be either injected or given orally.

A particular vaccine is given at a particular age this is called the immunization schedule.

Increasing Soil Fertility: After harvesting when the roots decompose, the nitrates present in the root nodules mix with the soil and enrich it with nitrogenous compounds and hence increasing the soil fertility.

sections.label: Harmful Microorganisms
sections.html:
Harmful Microorganisms: Microorganisms can be harmful in many ways. For example - some microorganisms cause diseases in human beings, other animals, and plants. Such microorganisms which cause diseases are called pathogens.

Malaria: Female Anopheles mosquito carries the parasite of malaria called Plasmodium.

Dengue: Female Aedes mosquito carries the dengue virus called Flavivirus.

Communicable diseases: Microbial diseases that can spread from an infected person to a healthy person through the air, water, food, or physical contact are called communicable diseases. Examples of such diseases include cholera, the common cold, chicken pox, and tuberculosis.

The communicable diseases can occur and spread in the following ways:

  • By breathing air containing microorganisms.
  • By taking infected food and water.
  • Through insect bites.
  • By sharing infected injection needles.
  • By physical contact with an infected person

There are some insects in our environment which transfer disease-causing microbes into our body and spread diseases such as mosquito and housefly. Such insect or other animal is called a carrier.

Prevention of diseases spread by houseflies:

  1. We should not leave household garbage here and there. This will prevent the houseflies from breeding because they will not be able to lay their eggs in the garbage.
  2. The food should always be kept covered so that flies cannot sit on it.
  3. We should avoid eating uncovered food items from roadside stalls.
  4. The flies should be killed by using insecticide spray and baits.

 

Disease-causing Microorganisms in Animals: Microorganisms cause diseases in animals too like in cows, buffalo, sheep, goats, and poultry birds. Some of the diseases caused in animals by microorganisms are – Foot and mouth disease, anthrax, and aspergillosis.

Foot and mouth disease: It is a viral disease in which the cattle get blisters on feet and mouth.

Anthrax: It is a bacterial disease caused by Bacillus anthracis.

Aspergillosis: It is a fungal disease.

Rust of wheat: It is caused by fungi. It is transmitted through air and seeds. It occurs in wheat plants.

Citrus canker: It is caused by bacteria. It is transmitted through the air. It occurs in citrus trees such as lemon, orange, etc.

Yellow vein mosaic of bhindi: It is a viral disease and is transmitted through insects.

sections.label: Food Preservation
sections.html:
Characteristics of spoilt food:

  1. Change of colour of food.
  2. Emission of bad smell from food.
  3. Change in appearance of food.
  4. Change in the test of food.
  5. Decomposition of food.
  6. Food surfaces have a slimy thread-like structure.
  7. Emission of gas.

 

Those that get spoilt easily by microorganisms are called perishable food materials such as cooked food, sweets fruits etc.

Those food materials that do not easily get spoilt by microorganisms are called non-perishable food materials, e.g. sugar, jiggery, flour, spices, etc.

Bacteriocidal Method of prevention:

  • Dehydration
  • Salting and pickling
  • Sugar syrup
  • Deep freezing
  • Chemical preservatives

Dehydration: The process of removal of water from a material is called dehydration. This reduces the quantity or percentage of moisture in it. It is done by sun drying. It is an old technique, used for cereals, pulses, spices, etc.

Salting and pickling: The process of preserving the food material by using salt, sugar, oil or vinegar. It is used for fish, meat, pickles, jams, jellies, vegetables, raw mango, chilies, amla, These preservations also give flavor to the food.

Sugar syrup: Concentrated sugar is used for the preservation of certain fruits, cheese, etc. A sugar syrup containing 70% of sugar does not allow bacterial growth, used for rasgulla, Gulab jamun, Squash, jam, jellies, etc.

Deep freezing: Keeping food materials at freezing temperatures below 0°C or water present in fruits and vegetables also freezer along with the frozen foodstuff is called deep freezing. At this temperature, bacteria become inactive and unable to grow and multiply. It is used for peas, meat, fish, chicken, etc.

Chemical preservatives: Certain chemical substances that are added to food to keep it safe and avoid spoilage are called chemical preservatives such as sodium benzoate and potassium metabisulfite.

Bacteriostaic Methods:

  • Irradiation
  • Canning
  • Pasteurization
  • Vacuum drying 

Irradiation: Treating or exposing the food to ionizing radiation such as X-rays and gamma rays to destroy the harmful microbes in it is called irradiation.

Canning: Storing of food materials in a metallic air-tight can after sterilization of cans is called canning.

Pasteurization: Many food materials in bottled e.g. milk. The milk is heated above 700°C and is suddenly cooled below 100°C. Most of the microorganisms are killed by this process. It is called pasteurization. This process was discovered by Louis Pasteur.

Vacuum drying: It is a scientific technique used for the preservation of milk by converting it into milk powder.

Expiry Date: The label on the packed food items has the manufacturing date, expiry date or best before or use within 2-4 months, etc, mentioned. This is to make the consumer/customer aware of self-life.

Advantages of food preservatives:

  1. It decreases the wastage of food by avoiding spoilage.
  2. It increases the storage period of food material.
  3. The nutritional value of the food is retained for a long period.
  4. It ensures food availability at distant places and during the off-season.

sections.label: Nitrogen Fixation
sections.html:
Nitrogen fixation is the process of conversion of atmospheric nitrogen into useful nitrogen compounds known as nitrogen fixation.

Nitrogen fixation is done in the following ways:

  1. Biological nitrogen fixation by some plants and microbes.
  2. Atmospheric nitrogen fixation by lightning.
  3. It is also done artificially by industrial nitrogen fixation in fertilizer factories.

Biological nitrogen fixation: The fixation of atmospheric nitrogen by living organisms into a usable form of nitrogen is called biological fixation.

Biological nitrogen fixation is carried by:

  1. Nitrogen-fixing bacteria, e.g. Rhizobium found in root nodules of leguminous plants such as peas.
  2. Nitrogen-fixing algae, e.g., blue-green algae- Nostoc found in paddy fields.
  3. Nitrogen-fixing non-leguminous plants, e.g. Alnus, Ginkgo, etc.

 

Atmospheric nitrogen fixation by lightning: It takes during the lighting in the sky. It helps to synthesize ammonia, and nitrates as nitrogenous compounds which are carried to earth or soil in rainwater.

Industrial Nitrogen Fixation: Nitrogen fixation is also done in industries. It is a process by which nitrogen-containing fertilizers are made in industries, e.g. urea. Therefore it is called industrial nitrogen.

sections.label: Nitrogen Cycle
sections.html:
Nitrogen cycle is the biogeochemical cycle that describes the transformation of nitrogen and nitrogen-containing compounds in nature.

Nitrogen Fixation: Atmospheric nitrogen is converted by lightning or certain bacteria like Rhizobium, Azotobacter, and blue-green algae (present in the soil) into compounds usable by plants.

Nitrification: Ammonia conversion into nitrites by Nitrosomonas and further conversion of nitrites into nitrates by Nitrobacter. Plants take up nitrogen in form of ammonia or nitrates.

Assimilation: Roots of plants absorb these nitrogenous compounds from soils and plants use them to synthesize proteins and other compounds.

Ammonification: When plants and animals die, bacteria and fungi present in the soil convert the nitrogenous wastes into compounds that can be used by plants again.

Denitrification: Nitrates can be converted into nitrogen gas which is released back into the atmosphere by certain bacteria. Eg. Pseudomonas.

sections.label: What are Synthetic Fibres?
sections.html:
There are two types of fiber: One which is obtained from natural sources and the other which is man-made.

Fibres that are obtained from natural sources are called natural fibers. For example - cotton, silk, wool, etc.

 

Fibers that are man-made are called man-made or synthetic fibers. For example - rayon, nylon, acrylic, etc.

A synthetic fibre, as well as plastic, is made up of a chain of small units (called Monomers) which combine to form polymers.

Monomers: A monomer is a single molecule that can bond with other identical molecules to form polymers through a process called Polymerization.

Polymers: Polymer is a Greek word in which 'poly' means 'many' and 'mer' means units. Hence, polymers are large molecules made up of several molecules (or monomers) linked together.

Natural fibres are also polymers. 'Cotton' is a polymer called 'Cellulose'.

‘Cellulose’ is made up of a number of single units (or monomers) called 'Glucose'.

Polymerization Reaction: The process of linking small monomers together to form polymers is called Polymerization.

Nylon is prepared by the polymerization of amide molecules.

Esters are chemicals which give fruits their smell.

sections.label: Types of Synthetic Fibres Rayon
sections.html:
Rayon is a versatile fibre and can imitate the feel and texture of silk, wool, cotton, and linen with drape and slipperiness akin to nylon.

Rayon resembles silk in appearance, texture, and shine. Hence, it is also known as Artificial Silk.

  • Rayon fibre can be dyed in different colours. 
  • Rayon is very cheap compared to silk.

Uses of rayon

  1. Rayon is used in the textile industry for making fabrics.
  2. Rayon is mixed with cotton to make bed sheets, dresses, aprons, and caps.
  3. Rayon is mixed with wool to make beautiful and durable carpets.
  4. Rayon is used to manufacturing tyre cords.
  5. Rayon is used to making bandages and lint for surgical dressing of wounds.

 

Nylon:

  • Nylon was the first commercially synthesized fibre.
  • Nylon is synthesized from coal, water, and air.
  • The fibre of nylon is stronger than a steel wire of the same thickness.
  • Nylon fibre was strong, elastic, and light. It was lustrous and easy to wash. So, it became very popular for making clothes.

Uses of Nylon:

  1. Due to the high tensile strength of the nylon fibre, it is used for making fishing nets, climbing ropes, parachute fibrics, strings for sports racquets, and musical instruments bristles for toothbrushes and paint brushes.
  2. Nylon is used in the production of textiles like sari, neckties, socks, and other garments.
  3. Wool blended with nylon is used to make socks, suits, and carpets.
  4. Nylon is used for making parts of a machine such as washers, pulleys, etc.

Polyester: Polyester is one of the most popular man-made fibres which are used in making clothes.

Polyester fibre does not get wrinkled easily. Hence, a fabric made from this fibre is easy to wash and does not need to be ironed - which makes it suitable for dress material.

Polyester is made up of two words - 'poly' which means many, and 'ester' which is a chemical.

Terylene (often known by brand name Dacron) can be drawn into very fine fibre and can be woven like any other yarn.

Polyethylene terephthalate (P.E.T.) is used for making wires, films, bottles, utensils and other products.

Uses of polyester

  • Polyester is used in making different types of apparel; such as shirts, pants, jackets, bed sheets, curtains, sarees, mouse-pad, etc.
  • Polyester is used in making ropes, fabrics for conveyor belts, cushioning and insulating material in pillows, etc.
  • It is used for making sails for sailboats.
  • Polyester is used for making conveyor belts.
  • Terry wool is used for making suits.

 

Acrylic is man-made fibre. Since acrylic resembles wool so it is also known as artificial wool or synthetic wool.

Acrylic fibre, fabric, plastic or paint are all made from acrylic acid.

The word 'acrylic' means 'containing acryl (or acrolein)' Acrolein is the sharp and bitter liquid in onions and has its roots in two Latin words - 'acer' which means 'sharp', and 'olere' which means 'to smell'.

Uses of Acrylic Fibre

  • Make woolen clothes like hats, scarves, gloves, sweaters, blankets, and other home-furnishing fabrics.
  • Make fake fur used for making toys and fur accessories.
  • Make garments for babies (as the fabric is machine-washable).

sections.label: Characteristics of Synthetic Fibres
sections.html:
Characteristic of Synthetic Fibre

  • Synthetic fibres are cheaper than natural fibre.
  • Synthetic fibres are stronger than natural fibre.
  • Synthetic fibres are more durable than natural fibre.
  • It is easy to maintain synthetic fibres. It is easy to wash the synthetic fabrics.

Characteristic of Synthetic Fibre

  • Synthetic fabrics are dried up in less time.
  • Synthetic fibres are readily available.
  • Synthetic fibres are fairly elastic and flexible as they can be stretched or compressed to some extent.

Characteristic of Synthetic Fibre

  • They are wrinkle-free and keep permanent creases.
  • Most of the synthetic fibres are not attacked by moths and moulds.
  • Most synthetic fibres are chemically inert the natural fibres.

Disadvantages of Synthetic Fibres

  1. Synthetic fibres cannot absorb moisture. This makes them unsuitable to be warm during summer because in summer when our body sweats. These fibres make the body sticky and sometimes cause skin irritation.
  2. They are dangerous to be worn near fire or heat, as they easily catch fire and are unfit to be wormed.
  3. They cannot be easily ironed as they melt very easily.

sections.label: Plastics
sections.html:
Plastic is a polymer (like synthetic fibre) that can be molded into different shapes.

The word ‘plastic’ originates from the Greek word ‘plastikos’ which means ‘that can be moulded or shaped’.

Plastic polymers can have different types of arrangement of monomers:

  • Linear Polymers
  • Branched Polymers
  • Cross-Linked Polymers
  • Network Polymers

Plastic can be recycled and used. It can also be melted, rolled into sheets, made into wires, and coloured.

Types of Plastic: Plastic can be divided into two main types - Thermoplastics and Thermosetting.

Thermoplastic: Plastics that can be easily bent or deform on heating are known as thermoplastic. PVC and Polythene are examples of thermoplastics.

Thermosetting plastic: Plastics that do not get deformed or softened on heating when molded once, are called thermosetting plastics. Bakelite and melamine are examples of thermosetting plastics.

  • Thermosetting plastics are used in making hard boards, electric switch, handles of electrical appliances, handles of kitchen utensils, floor tiles, etc.
  • Thermoplastics are used in making toys, bottles, combs, containers, etc.

Melamine is a poor conductor of heat and resists fire, thus it is used in making floor tiles, kitchen materials, etc.

Bakelite is a poor conductor of electricity and heat, thus it is used in making electrical switches, and other electrical appliances.

sections.label: Plastics as Materials of Choice
sections.html:
Plastic is a Poor conductor: Plastic is a poor conductor of heat and electricity. This makes it suitable to make covering electric wires, handles of electrical appliances, handles of utensils, kitchenware, floor tiles, etc.

Plastic is non-reactive: Plastic does not react with air and water and with many chemicals. Since plastic does not react with air and water hence it does not get rusted like iron. This character makes plastic suitable for making containers, water tanks, water bottles, plastic pipes, taps, chairs, tables, and other many types of furniture.

Plastic is strong and durable: Plastics are lightweight, durable, cheap, and very strong. These qualities of plastic made it the need of today. Whether it is polythene bags or pencil boxes, water bottles or umbrellas, furniture or aircraft, the use of plastic can be seen everywhere.

Melamine is used for making unbreakable dinnerware, floor tiles, decorative objects, fire-resistant fabrics, and kitchenware.

Polythene is used for making carry bags, adhesive tapes, and pipes to transport liquids.

PVC is used for making floor tiles, durable toys, furniture, and bottle.

Plastic is inert to chemicals: Teflon is used for making non-stick cookware as it is a special type of plastic on which oil and water do not stick.

Plastic is insoluble in water: Containers used to store water are made up of plastic.

Plastic is highly inflammable: Plastics burn easily to produce harmful gases which pollute our environment.

sections.label: Plastics and the Environment
sections.html:
Substances that get decomposed through natural processes, such as the action of bacteria, etc. are called biodegradable substances. For Example; potato peels, peels of other vegetables, foodstuffs, fruit, paper, cotton cloths, wood, etc.

Substances that either does not decompose or take many years to get decomposed through the natural process are called non-biodegradable substance, e.g. tin, aluminium, plastics, etc.

Plastic is a non-biodegradable substance. If it is left or thrown, it takes many years to get decomposed or either does not get decomposed. The non-biodegradable nature of plastic has made it a very major problem for the environment.

Rs to Minimize the Environmental Damage caused by Plastic

Refuse: Do not buy things we do not need.

Reduce: Minimize the use of plastics in our daily lives.

Reuse: Reuse the items we already have instead of throwing them away.

Repurpose: If we are not using something, alter or change to use it differently.

Recycle: Some plastic waste can be sorted and made into other things in recycling factories. This helps us to reduce our carbon footprint.

How can we minimize pollution caused by plastics at home?
We should use jute, cotton, or paper bags instead of plastics. We also need to minimize the use of plastic in our daily lives (such as using a steel lunch box instead of a plastic one). Also, we should not throw plastics on the road or in the water bodies.

sections.label: Physical Properties of Metals and Non-metals
sections.html:
Elements can be classified into two groups depending on their physical and chemical properties:

  • Metals
  • Non-metals

Metals: The elements which are hard, and shiny can be beaten into sheets, drawn into wires, and are good conductors of heat and electricity are generally metals. For example iron, copper, gold, etc.

Minerals from which metals can be profitably extracted are called ores. For example, calcium occurs in limestone (calcium carbonate) or iron in the ore haematite.

Non-metals do not have properties possessed by metals. Examples of non-metals are sulphur, carbon, oxygen, phosphorus, etc

Metalloids: The elements which possess the properties of metals and non-metals are called metalloids. For example boron, silicon, germanium, tellurium, arsenic, and antimony are metalloids.

The property of metals by which they can be beaten into thin sheets is called malleability.

The property of metal by which metals can be drawn into wires is called ductility.

Sonorous means capable of producing a ringing sound.

Brittle means which breaks into pieces on hammering or stretching.

Physical Properties of Metals and Non-metals:
Hardness-

  • Metals are generally hard but their hardness varies from metal to metal. It is difficult to cut a sheet of iron due to its hardness. Sodium and potassium are metals that are soft and can be easily cut with a knife.
  • Most of the non-metals are very soft and can be cut easily. Example - sulphur and phosphorous. Diamond is a non-metal which is very hard. It is the hardest natural substance known.

Physical Properties of Metals and Non-metals:
Malleability-

  • Metals are malleable which means they can be beaten into thin sheets with a hammer. Most of the metals are malleable. Gold and silver are the best malleable metals. Aluminium and copper are also highly malleable. Iron is also malleable that is why it can be hammered into iron sheets. Aluminium is used to make foils.
    ​​​​​​​
  • On the other hand, non-metals are not malleable. They are brittle and break into small pieces when hammered. Example - a pencil lead is made up of graphite (carbon) which is a non-metal so when it is hammered, it breaks into small pieces.

Physical Properties of Metals and Non-metals:
Ductility-

  • Metals are ductile which means they can be drawn into thin wires. Gold and silver are the best ductile metals. Other metals such as iron, aluminium, copper, etc. are also ductile.
  • On the other hand, non-metals are not ductile. They cannot be stretched into wires. Example - sulphur and phosphorous, when stretched breaks into small pieces.

Physical Properties of Metals and Non-metals:
Conductivity-

  • Metals are good conductors of heat and electricity. This means they allow heat and electricity to pass through them easily. Copper, gold, iron, silver, aluminium, etc. are all metals which are good conductors of heat that is why we cannot hold a hot utensil without plastic or a wooden handle.
  • Non-metals are poor conductors of heat and electricity. They do not allow heat and electricity to pass through them. Example – sulphur does not conduct heat and electricity.

Physical Properties of Metals and Non-metals:
Luster-

  • Metals are lustrous. They have shiny appearance that is why they are used in making jewellery. Example – gold and silver.
    ​​​​​​​
  • Non-metals have dull appearance. Only iodine has a shiny appearance.
Physical Properties of Metals and Non-metals:
Sonorousness-
  • Metals are sonorous which means that they produce a ringing sound when we strike them. That is why they are used to make bells.
  • If we drop a metal coin on the floor of our house, we hear a ringing sound.
  • Non-metals are not sonorous. If a piece of carbon or sulphur is dropped on the floor, we do not hear any ringing sound.

sections.label: Chemical Properties of Metals and Non-metals
sections.html:
The reaction of metal with oxygen:
Metals react with oxygen to form metal oxides. Metal oxides are basic in nature which turns red litmus to blue.

When magnesium burns in air, it combines with oxygen to form magnesium oxide.

2Mg (s) + o2 (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2MgO (s)

Reaction of non-metal with oxygen:
Non-metals react with oxygen to form non-metal oxides which are acidic in nature and turn blue litmus red. When sulphur burns in air, it combines with oxygen to form sulphur dioxide.
S(s) + O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} SO2(g)

Reaction of metal with water:
When a metal reacts with water, then a metal hydroxide and hydrogen gas are formed. The vigor of the reaction of metal with water depends on its chemical reactivity. For example – sodium is a very reactive metal so it reacts violently even with cold water.
{tex}\underset{Sodium}{2{\mathrm{Na}}_{(\mathrm s)}}+\underset{\mathrm{Water}}{2{\mathrm H}_2{\mathrm O}_{(\mathrm l)}}\longrightarrow\underset{Sodium\;hydroxide}{2{\mathrm{NaOH}}_{(\mathrm{aq})}}+\underset{Hydrogen}{{\mathrm H}_{2(\mathrm g)}\uparrow}{/tex}

Reaction of non-metal with water:
Non-metals do not react with water. For example - sulphur is a non-metal which does not react with water.

Reaction of metal with acids:
Most of the metals react with dilute acids to form salts and hydrogen gas that burns with a ‘pop’ sound. Only less reactive metals like copper, silver, and gold do not react with dilute acids.

Magnesium reacts with dilute hydrochloric acid to form magnesium chloride and hydrogen gas.

Magnesium + Hydrochloric acid {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Magnesium chloride + Hydrogen
Mg + 2HCl {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} MgCl2 + H2{tex}\uparrow{/tex}

Reaction of non-metal with acids:
Non-metals generally do not react with acids to form salts and hydrogen gas.

Reaction of metal with bases:
Some metals react with bases to form salts and hydrogen gas.  For example – when aluminum is heated with sodium hydroxide solution, then sodium aluminate and hydrogen gas are formed.

Reaction of non-metal with bases:
Some non-metals react with bases but no hydrogen gas is produced. Their reactions are quite complex.

Reactivity series of metals:
The arrangement of metals in the vertical column in the order of decreasing reactivities is called the reactivity series of metals.

Displacement reactions:
In displacement reactions, one metal displaces another metal from its compound in an aqueous solution. A more reactive metal displaces a less reactive metal from its salt solution.

Reaction of iron metal with copper sulphate solution: When a strip of iron metal is placed in copper sulphate solution for some time, then the blue colour of the copper sulphate solution fades and a red-brown coating of copper metal is deposited on the iron strip.

Reaction of zinc metal with copper sulphate solution: When a strip of zinc metal is placed in copper sulphate solution for some time, then the blue colour of copper sulphate solution fades gradually and a red-brown coating of copper metal is deposited on the zinc strip. The reaction is as follows:
{tex}\underset{(Blue)}{Copper\;Sulphate\;(CuSO_4)\;}+\;\underset{(Silvery\;White)}{Zinc\;(Zn)\;}\rightarrow\;{/tex} {tex}\underset{(Colourless)}{Zinc\;Sulphate\;(ZnSO_4)\;}+\;\underset{(Red)}{Copper\;(Cu)}{/tex}

sections.label: Uses of Metals and Non-metals
sections.html:
Uses of Metals:

  1. Iron, copper and aluminium metals are used for making cooking utensils.
  2. Copper is used in making electrical wires for household wiring, electric motors, etc.
  3. Aluminium foils are used for packaging medicines, chocolates, food items and many other materials.
  4. Aluminium metal is used to make aeroplanes.
  5. Iron metal is used to make nails, railings, water tanks, industrial tools, and transport vehicles like cars, buses, and trucks.
  6. Mercury is used in making thermometers.

Uses of Non-metals:

  1. Oxygen is used by plants and animals including human beings for breathing.
  2. Nitrogen is used in making fertilisers to enhance the growth of plants.
  3. Chlorine is used in water purification.
  4. Iodine is used to make tincture iodine which is applied on cuts and wounds as an antiseptic.
  5. Sulphur and phosphorous are used in making crackers.

sections.label: Coal
sections.html:
Fuel: Fuel is any substance that releases large amounts of energy in the form of heat and light when it is burned. For example, coal and petroleum are fuels.

Fuels can be classified as:

Classification of Fuels Definition Examples
Solid Fuels These fuels exist in a solid state at room temperature For example, coal, firewood, animal dung cakes, charcoal, coke
Liquid Fuels These are volatile liquids that produce vapour which burns and produces energy For example, petrol, diesel, kerosene
Gaseous Fuel These are the fuels that exist in the form of a gas or as a mixture of different gaseous fuels. For example, natural gas, biogas, CNG, coal gas

Inexhaustible natural resources: These natural resources are present in unlimited quantity in nature and are not likely to be exhausted by human activities. Their supply is never ending and can be used again and again. Examples are sunlight, water, and air.

Exhaustible natural resources: These natural resources are present in limited quantity in nature and can be exhausted by human activities. Example – natural gas, minerals, forests, wildlife, etc. These resources do not last forever.

Fossil fuels are fuels that are formed by fossils (or dead remains) of living organisms.

Need fossil fuels:

  • Transportation: Fuel is used to run different vehicles such as cars, trucks, motorcycles, trains, airplanes, etc.
  • Cooking: fuels are needed for cooking. For instance, LPG is used in urban areas while firewood, coal, and cattle dung is used in rural areas.
  • Heating: fuel is also used to generate heat.
  • Electricity production: different fuels such as coal, petrol, natural gas, and diesel are used to generate electricity in power plants.
  • Industrial usage: many industries use fuels for different purposes such as producing electricity, running their machinery, and heating.

The characteristics of good fuel are:

  • It should be economical.
  • It should be available easily
  • It should not emit poisonous gases on burning
  • It should be easy to transport and handle
  • It should not produce a bad odour

Coal: Coal is a hard, black, combustible mineral that consists mainly of carbon. It is found in deep coal mines under the surface of the earth.

Coal is a fossil fuel formed from dead plant matter over millions of years due to heat and pressure.

Coal consists of hydrogen, carbon and oxygen, and sulphur (small amount).

Coal is extracted from the ground through a process called mining.
Coal Mining can be of two types:

  • Opencast Mining: Mining in which coal is extracted from near the earth’s surface.
  • Underground Mining: Mining in which coal is extracted from deep inside the earth’s crust.

Uses of coal:

  1. Coal is used as a fuel in homes and industry.
  2. It is used as a fuel in Thermal Power Plants for generating electricity.
  3. It is used to make coal gas which is an important industrial fuel.
  4. Coal is used to make coke, coal tar, and coal gas.
  5. Earlier, coal was used as a fuel to make steam to run the engines of trains.

 

Products of coal: When coal is heated strongly in the absence of air, it produces coke, coal tar, and coal gas.

Coke: Coke is a tough and porous black solid substance that is produced by heating coal in the absence of air. It is 98% carbon.

Uses of coke:

  • It is used in the manufacture of steel.
  • It is used in the extraction of many metals like iron, zinc, etc.
  • It is used as fuel.

 

Coal tar: Coal tar is a thick, black liquid having an unpleasant smell which is obtained by heating coal in the absence of air. It is a mixture of 200 carbon compounds which includes benzene, toluene, phenol, aniline, anthracene, and naphthalene.

Uses of Coal tar:

  • Coal tar is used to make synthetic fibres, drugs, plastics, synthetic dyes, paints, pesticides, etc.
  • It has been traditionally used for metalling roads. These days, bitumen, a petroleum product, is used in place of coal tar for metalling the roads.
  • Naphthalene balls used to repel moths and other insects are also obtained from coal tar.

 

Coal gas: Coal gas is a gaseous fuel that is obtained by strong heating of coal in the absence of air during the processing of coal to get a coke.

Uses of coal gas:

  • Coal gas burns, it produces a good amount of light. So, in the past, it has been used for lighting purposes.
  • Coal gas was used for street lighting for the first time in London in 1810 and in New York around 1820. Nowadays, it is used as a source of heat rather than light.
  • It is used as a fuel in many industries situated near coal processing plants.

 

Substances are released on the burning of coal

  • Carbon dioxide
  • Carbon monoxide
  • Sulphur Dioxide
  • Nitrogen
  • Lead
  • Arsenic
  • Mercury

sections.label: Petroleum
sections.html:
The term 'Petroleum' is derived from two words - 'Petra' which means 'rock' and 'oleum' which means 'oil'. It is mined from the rocks under the Earth.

Petroleum is a dark coloured, thick crude oil found deep below the ground in certain areas. It has an unpleasant smell.

Petroleum was formed by the decomposition of the remains of the tiny plants and animals buried under the sea millions of years ago.

First Oil Well in the World was drilled in Pennsylvania, USA in 1859.

The second Oil Well in the World was drilled in Makum, Assam, India in 1867.

In India, the oil is found in:

  • Assam,
  • Gujarat,
  • Mumbai High, and
  • River basins of Godavari and Krishna.

Petroleum is a mixture of various constituents such as petroleum gas, petrol, diesel, lubricating oil, paraffin wax, etc.

The process of separating the various constituents or fractions of petroleum is known as refining. It is carried out in a petroleum refinery. The separation of petroleum into different fractions is done by the process of fractional distillation.

S.NO. Constituents of Petroleum Uses
i. Petroleum gas Fuel for home and industry.
ii. Petrol Fuel for running vehicles such as cars, scooters, etc., aviation fuel, and solvent for dry cleaning.
iii. Kerosene Fuel for stoves, lamps, and aviation fuel in jet aeroplanes.
iv. Diesel Fuel for heavy motor vehicles such as trucks, buses, etc., and in electric generators.
v. Lubricating oil Lubrication in machines and engines.
vi. Paraffin wax Making candles, ointments, vaseline, wax paper, and grease.
vii. Bitumen Road surfacing, making black paints.
  • Petroleum is called 'black gold' because it yields several substances that are commercially successful.
  • The useful substances obtained from petroleum and natural gas are called 'petrochemicals'.

sections.label: Natural Gas
sections.html:
Natural gas is a fossil fuel found naturally as a hydrocarbon gas mixture in oil wells.

Natural gas mainly consists of methane with small quantities of ethane and propane. When natural gas is compressed by applying pressure, it is called Compressed Natural Gas (CNG).

Advantages of using Natural gas or CNG:

  1. Natural gas (CNG) is a good fuel because it burns easily and produces a lot of heat. It burns with a smokeless flame and causes no air pollution.
  2. Natural gas (CNG) is a complete fuel in itself and can be used directly for heating purposes in homes and industries. There is no need to add anything else to it.
  3. The great advantage of CNG is that it can be used directly for burning in homes and factories where it can be supplied through pipes. Such a network of pipelines exist in Vadodara (Gujarat), some parts of Delhi, and other places.

 

Uses of Natural gas (CNG):

  1. Natural gas is used as a domestic and industrial fuel.
  2. It is used as a fuel in Thermal Power Stations for generating electricity.
  3. Compressed natural gas (CNG) is being used increasingly as a fuel in transport vehicles like cars, buses, etc. in place of petrol and diesel.
  4. Natural gas is used as a source of hydrogen gas needed to manufacture fertilizers.

In India, the Petroleum Conservation Research Association (PCRA) advises people how to save petrol or diesel while driving.

In India, vast reserves of natural gas are found in:

  • Maharashtra,
  • Rajasthan,
  • Tripura, and
  • River Delta of Krishna and Godavari.

sections.label: Some Natural Resources are Limited
sections.html:
Fossil fuels, such as coal, petroleum, and natural gas cannot be created in the laboratory as it is not possible to create the natural conditions under which they are formed. Moreover, it takes thousands of years for them to be formed.

Consequences of the burning of fossil fuels
Increase in air pollution: The burning of fossil fuels results in the release of unburnt carbon particles in the air. These particles act as pollutants and increase air pollution. Fossil fuels release poisonous gases such as carbon monoxide and sulphur dioxide into the atmosphere.
Global Warming: Fossil fuels when burnt release large amounts of carbon dioxide into the air. As the amount of carbon dioxide increases it results in an increase in the global temperature of the earth and leads to global warming.

Why should we use fossil fuels economically?

  • They are available in limited quantities.
  • Burning these fuels also causes air pollution as well as global warming.

 

According to the Petroleum Conservation Research Association (PCRA) in India, we can save petrol or diesel while driving by:

  • Drive at a constant and moderate speed.
  • Maintain tyre pressure at correct levels.
  • Switch off the engine when you are waiting( such as a traffic light).
  • Do regular maintenance of your vehicle.

 

Why fossil fuels can last for 100 years only?

  • Fossil fuels are exhaustible resources which means that they are not present in abundant quantities on the earth.
  • They take hundreds and thousands of years to replenish.
  • The rate at which they are being consumed today is increasing at a rapid pace.
  • It means that more amounts of fossil fuels are being used than it is being replenished.
  • Hence, scientists claim that they can replenish completely in 100 years.

sections.label: What is Combustion?
sections.html:
A chemical process in which a substance reacts with oxygen to give off heat and light is called combustion. The burning of wood is an example of combustion.

Substances that easily catch fire are called combustible substances. For example paper, coal, and wood.

Substances that do not catch fire readily are non-combustible substances. For example sand, water, and glass.

Inflammable substances are substances that have low ignition temperatures and catch fire easily. They burn with a flame. For example, petrol, LPG, etc.

When we eat food, it gets broken down into simpler substances that react with oxygen and generate energy (or heat). Hence, food is referred to as ‘fuel’ for our body.

Conditions necessary for combustion:

  1. Presence of a combustible substance.
  2. Presence of a supporter of combustion.
  3. Heating the combustible substance to its ignition temperature.

sections.label: How Do We Control Fire?
sections.html:
Combustion requires fuel: Combustion only takes place when there is a fuel that produces heat and light when it catches fire. For example, petrol and diesel.

Combustion requires air: We can prove it with the help of the following experiment: A candle keeps burning in the open air. However, when we cover it with a glass or a jar, it only burns until the oxygen inside it is consumed. Once the oxygen is exhausted, the flame of the candle flickers off.

Combustion requires heat: The substance must reach its ignition temperature to catch fire.

Ignition temperature is the lowest temperature at which a substance catches fire or starts burning.

Any fire needs three things to be present - fuel, air (oxygen), and heat. If any one of these three things is removed, then the burning will stop and the fire will be extinguished.

The job of a fire extinguisher is to cut off the supply of air or bring down the temperature of the fuel, or both.

Water is the most common fire extinguisher. But it works only when things like wood and paper are on fire. If electrical equipment is on fire, water may conduct electricity and cause a fire. Water is also not suitable for fires involving oil and petrol.

For fires involving electrical equipment and inflammable materials like petrol, carbon dioxide (CO2) is the best extinguisher as it is heavier than oxygen and covers the fire like a blanket. Since the contact between the fuel and oxygen is cut off, the fire is controlled.

Fire extinguisher: Carbon dioxide can be stored at high pressure as a liquid in cylinders. When CO2 is released from the cylinder, it expands enormously in volume and cools down. So, it not only forms a blanket around the fire but also brings down the temperature of the fuel. That is why it is an excellent fire extinguisher.

We can control the fire by one or more of the following:

  • Removing the fuel
  • Cutting off the air supply (or oxygen supply)
  • Cutting off heat or lowering the temperature of the fuel

sections.label: Types of Combustion
sections.html:
Rapid Combustion: Combustion in which a gas burns quickly producing heat and light in the process. E.g. LPG.

Spontaneous Combustion: Combustion in which a material bursts into flames suddenly without applying heat. E.g. Phosphorus which burns at room temperature. Spontaneous combustion of coal dust often causes accidental fires in coal mines. The heat from the sun or lighting may also cause spontaneous forest fires.

Explosion: When a material bursts suddenly to produce heat, light, and sound on the application of heat or pressure, it is called an explosion. E.g. Crackers and fireworks release a large amount of gas too.

sections.label: Flame
sections.html:
A flame is a region where the combustion or burning of a gaseous substance takes place.

Only the solid or liquids that form gases when heated burn with a flame.

Some of the substances which burn by producing flames are LPG, biogas, candle wax, camphor, magnesium, and mustard oil.

Charcoal and coal do not burn by producing a flame.

When fuels burn, the type of flame produced depends on the proportion of oxygen available. When the oxygen supply is insufficient, then the fuels burn incompletely and produce mainly a yellow flame and when the oxygen supply is sufficient, then the fuels burn completely and produce mainly a blue flame.

sections.label: Structure of a Flame
sections.html:
The outermost zone is blue in colour and is the hottest part of the flame. This is also the zone where complete combustion takes place

The Middle zone is yellow in colour and is moderately hot. In this zone, partial combustion takes place.

The innermost zone is black in colour and the least hot. Here, we can find the unburned wax vapours of a candle.

The outermost zone of a flame is its hottest part. Gold and silver have high melting points and hence, goldsmiths blow at the outermost zone of the flame to melt gold and silver quickly.

sections.label: What is a Fuel?
sections.html:
Fuels are substances that give us heat that we use for domestic and industrial purposes, such as wood, kerosene, and petrol.

Characteristics of an ideal fuel:

  1. It has a high calorific value.
  2. It burns easily in the air at a moderate rate.
  3. It has a proper ignition temperature.
  4. It does not produce any harmful gases or leaves any residue after burning.

sections.label: Fuel Efficiency
sections.html:
The fuel efficiency of a fuel depends on its calorific value.

The calorific value of a fuel depends on the amount of heat produced by the complete combustion of 1 kg of the fuel.

The unit used to measure the calorific value of a fuel is kilojoule per kg (kJ/kg).

Calorific Value of Common Fuels We Use

Fuel Calorific Value (Kj/kg)
Cow dung cake 6000 - 8000
Wood 17000 - 22000
Coal 25000 - 33000
Petrol 45000
Kerosene 45000
Diesel 45000
Methane 50000
CNG 50000
LPG 55000
Biogas 35000 - 40000
Hydrogen 150000

Uses of Fuels

  • Gasoline is used in cars, scooters, and other vehicles we use every day.
  • Natural gas is used in heating systems, water heaters, dryers, and stovetops in our homes.
  • Oil and natural gas are used in making several things that we use every day. Hydrocarbons, for example, are used in making plastics, pharmaceuticals, and several other items we use daily.
  • Coal is the primary fossil fuel used in many thermal power plants to produce electricity.

Liquid Fuels: Petroleum (which is a fossil fuel), crude oil (from which we get petrol or gasoline), diesel, kerosene oil, etc.

Solid Fuels: Firewood, charcoal, coal (fossil fuel which is mined as steam coke or soft coke), dung cakes, tallow (animal fat), straw and other agricultural wastes, paraffin wax, camphor, etc.

Gaseous Fuels: Most commonly used gas is LPG (Liquid Petroleum Gas) which we use as cooking gas at home.

The important harmful effects are:

  • The burning of fuels like wood, coal, petroleum products, diesel, etc. releases unburnt carbon particles in the air which causes respiratory diseases such as asthma. 
  • Incomplete combustion of fuels produces a very poisonous gas called carbon monoxide which can kill a person if inhaled excessively.
  • The burning of fuels releases carbon dioxide into the air in the environment. An increased percentage of carbon dioxide causes global warming.
  • The burning of coal, petrol, and diesel produce sulphur dioxide gas which goes into the air. It is extremely suffocating and corrosive in nature.

 

Global warming is the rising temperature of the Earth's atmosphere which is causing the melting down of glaciers making the sea levels rise and causing floods in coastal areas.

Global warming is caused due to the elevated levels of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere as CO2 absorbs infrared radiation emitted by the Earth and radiates it back to the atmosphere and raises its temperature. This is also known as the ‘greenhouse effect.

Acid Rain: The rain of acids is very harmful to crops, soil, animals, and buildings. These acids are formed when sulphur and nitrogen oxides dissolve in rainwater. Acid rains also damage trees at high elevation, causes acidification of lake or stream water, and damage forest soils.

Irreplaceable buildings, statues, and sculptures which are part of a nation’s cultural heritage can get destroyed due to acid rain.

sections.label: Deforestation and Its Causes
sections.html:
Biodiversity: The variety of organisms that exist on the Earth, their interrelationships, and their relationship with their environment is called Biological Diversity and Biodiversity.

Deforestation: means clearing forests for different purposes, such as timber and other uses of land.

Causes of deforestation are:

  1. The forest trees are cut down to obtain wood for use as fuel.
  2. The trees are cut down to obtain wood for making doors, windows, and furniture.
  3. Wood is required for making paper.
  4. The forest trees are cut down to obtain more agricultural land for the cultivation of crops.
  5. The forest trees are cut down to get land for building houses, factories, roads, and dams.

 

Some natural causes of deforestation are forest fires and severe droughts.

sections.label: Consequences of Deforestation
sections.html:
Deforestation causes:

  • Increase in the pollution level of the Earth
  • Increase in carbon dioxide in the atmosphere
  • Increase in temperature or global warming
  • Lowering the level of groundwater
  • Disturbance in the balance of Nature
  • Decrease in the rainfall
  • Increase in the changes of natural calamities (such as droughts and floods)

The deforestation will have the following consequences for all living organisms:

  • Deforestation will lead to a shortage of wood and other forest products.
  • It will cause an increase in the temperature of the earth’s atmosphere leading to global warming.
  • It will cause soil erosion making the soil infertile and leading to desertification.

 

The deforestation will have the following consequences for all living organisms:

  • Deforestation will cause frequent flooding of rivers leading to loss of life and property.
  • It affects the water cycle leading to a decrease in rainfall which could cause droughts and thus lower the groundwater level.
  • Deforestation leads to the extinction of many wild animals and plants.

Carbon dioxide traps heat rays reflected by the Earth which increases in temperature on the Earth and leads to global warming.

Deforestation and increase in temperature on the Earth disturb the water cycle, which leads to a reduction in rainfall and droughts.

Desertification is the process by which fertile land turns into a desert. Deforestation is a major cause of desertification as it exposes the top layer of soil to natural forces like wind, water, and sunlight.

Due to desertification:

  • the soil erosion takes place
  • physical properties of the soil change and it becomes less fertile (its nutrient content and texture changes)
  • the water holding capacity of the soil decreases which may lead to floods.

sections.label: Conservation of Forest and Wildlife
sections.html:
Measures that can be taken to conserve forests and wildlife are:

  1. The unauthorized cutting of forest trees for timber trade and fire-wood should be stopped immediately.
  2. In the case of Government authorized felling of trees, for every acre of forest cut down, an equal area of land should be planted with saplings of trees to make up for the loss in long run.
  3. The natural habitats of wild animals should be preserved by establishing conservation areas such as Biosphere Reserves, Wildlife Sanctuaries, and National Parks.
  4. A total ban should be imposed on the poaching or capturing of any wild animal or bird.

 

There are three types of protected areas that have been established by the Government for the conservation of forests and wild animals.

  1. Wildlife Sanctuary
  2. National Park
  3. Biosphere Reserve

Wildlife Sanctuary: A Wildlife Sanctuary is a protected area of land which is created for the protection of wild animals in their natural environment like forests.

National Park: A National Park is a relatively large area of scenic beauty protected and maintained by the government to preserve flora and fauna, landscape, historic objects of the area and the places of scientific interest.

Biosphere Reserve: A Biosphere Reserve is a large, protected area of land meant for the conservation of wildlife, biodiversity, and the traditional lifestyle of the tribal people living in that area.

Wildlife Conservation is the practice of protecting endangered plant animal species and their habitats in an effort to maintain the ecological balance.

sections.label: Biosphere Reserve
sections.html:
Biosphere: Part of the Earth that supports life is called the biosphere. It is here that all living organisms exist.

Biosphere Reserve: Biosphere reserves are larger areas of land which aim to conserve the wildlife as well as the traditional life of the tribal living in the area (allowing them to use the plant and animal resources to some extent).

A Biosphere Reserve is divided into three zones – core zone, buffer zone and transition zone.

The innermost zone of a Biosphere Reserve is known as core zone which is devoted to strict protection of wildlife.

The middle zone of a Biosphere Reserve is called buffer zone which surrounds the core zone and only limited human activity is allowed in it.

The outermost zone of a Biosphere Reserve is called the transition zone in which several non-destructive human activities such as settlements of tribals and cultivation of crops, etc., are allowed.

A biosphere reserve may also contain other protected areas in it. For example - A Biosphere Reserve may contain Wildlife Sanctuary or a National Park.

The Pachmarhi Biosphere Reserve consists of one national park named Satpura and two wildlife sanctuaries named Bori and Pachmarhi.

The names and locations of some of the Biosphere Reserves of India are:

  1. Great Nicobar Biosphere Reserve (Andaman and Nicobar)
  2. Kaziranga Biosphere Reserve (Assam)
  3. Sunderbans Biosphere Reserve (West Bengal)
  4. Kanha Biosphere Reserve (Madhya Pradesh)
  5. Pachmarhi Biosphere Reserve (Madhya Pradesh)

Role of Biosphere Reserves:

  • They help in the conservation of wildlife and maintain the biodiversity of the area.
  • They preserve the natural ecological conditions in the area.
  • They promote the economic development of the area and maintain the lifestyle of tribal people living in that area.
  • They provide opportunities for scientific research, environmental education, and tourism.

 

There are 18 biosphere reserves in India, which are:

S. No. Name State
10 Achanakmar-Amarkantak Biosphere Reserve Madhya Pradesh, Chhattisgarh
14 Agasthyamalai Biosphere Reserve Kerala, Tamil Nadu
12 Cold Desert Himachal Pradesh
16 Dibru-Saikhowa Assam

17 Seshachalam Hills  Andhra Pradesh
7 Simlipal Odisha
5 Sundarbans West Bengal

sections.label: Flora and Fauna
sections.html:
Flora: Plants found in a particular area are referred to as the flora of the area. For example - Teak, Jamun, Fern, Mango, and Arjun are the flora of Pachmarhi Biosphere Reserve.

Fauna: Animals found in a particular area are referred to as the fauna of the area. Cheetal, wolf, leopard, Blue bull, Barking deer, and Wild dog are the fauna of Pachmarhi Biosphere Reserve.

sections.label: Endemic Species
sections.html:
Species: A group of living organisms that can interbreed with each other is called a Species.

Endangered Species: Species whose number diminishes so much that they might face extinction (or vanish off the face of the Earth) are known as Endangered Species. There can be endangered animals as endangered plants.

Endemic Species: Species of plants and animals that are exclusively found in a particular area are called Endemic to that zone, state, or country. The endemic species are not found anywhere else naturally. The endemic fauna of the Pachmarhi Biosphere Reserve includes bison, Indian giant squirrel, and flying squirrel.

Endemic flora of the Pachmarhi Biosphere Reserve includes sal and wild mango.

Giant squirrel, flying squirrel and bison are the endemic fauna of Pachmarhi Biosphere Reserve.

sections.label: Wildlife Sanctuary
sections.html:
Wildlife Sanctuary: This is a protected area where animals can live in their natural habitat without any disturbance.

A Wildlife Sanctuary is a protected area of land which is created for the protection of wild animals in their natural environment like forests. It is a place where wild animals remain safe.

In wildlife sanctuaries, poaching (killing) or capturing of wild animals is strictly prohibited. Some of the threatened wild animals which are protected and preserved in Wildlife Sanctuaries are black buck, white-eyed buck, elephant, golden cat, pink-headed duck, gharial, marsh crocodile, python, rhinoceros, etc.

Some of the Wildlife Sanctuaries in India are:

  1. Sanjay Gandhi Wildlife Sanctuary (Maharashtra)
  2. Mudumalai Wildlife Sanctuary (Tamilnadu)
  3. Nagarjunsagar Wildlife Sanctuary (Andhra Pradesh)
  4. Bharatpur Bird Sanctuary (Rajasthan)
  5. Sultanpur Lake Bird Sanctuary (Haryana)

People living in Wildlife Sanctuaries can:

  • Graze livestock
  • Collect firewood or medicinal plants

Activities prohibited in wildlife sanctuaries include:

  • Killing (poaching) animals
  • Capturing animals

Jim Corbett was the first tiger reserve in India. It is situated in Uttarakhand and is also the oldest national park in India.

sections.label: National Park
sections.html:
National Park: This area is reserved for wildlife allowing them to freely use the available habitats and natural resources.

A National Park is a relatively large area of scenic beauty protected and maintained by the government to preserve flora and fauna, landscape, historic objects of the area and places of scientific interest.
